prompt:
You are distilling Chad Fowler's blog about AI-era software architecture (the "Phoenix Architecture") into an LLM-friendly reference document.

SOURCE: A local HTTrack mirror at /Users/matteo/dojo/2026-07-22-phoenix/aicoding-mirror — the actual pages are under the aicoding.leaflet.pub/ subdirectory. Explore it, find ALL the individual blog posts (not just the index page), and read each one fully. The HTML may have boilerplate/navigation cruft; extract the actual article text. Watch out for hts-cache and other HTTrack bookkeeping — ignore those.

OUTPUT: Write a single markdown file to /Users/matteo/dojo/2026-07-22-phoenix/todomvc/docs/principles.md

PURPOSE OF THE OUTPUT (this shapes how you write it): This document will be loaded into the context of an LLM agent whose job is to (a) help write/maintain a behavioral spec and black-box eval suite for an application, and (b) regenerate the application's implementation from scratch from that spec + evals whenever the spec changes. The document is a durable artifact; the code is disposable. Write for that consumer, not for a human casual reader.

FORMAT REQUIREMENTS:
- Start with a short provenance header: source URL (https://aicoding.leaflet.pub/), author, date distilled (2026-07-22), and a one-line disclaimer that this is a distillation.
- Then a "Core thesis" section: 3-6 sentences max.
- Then a "Principles" section: each principle as a bolded imperative one-liner followed by 1-3 sentences of explanation. Prefer operational phrasing ("Do X", "Never Y") over abstract phrasing. Where the blog has a memorable exact phrase, quote it verbatim in quotes with the post title it came from.
- Then a "Practices" section: concrete workflow guidance the blog actually gives about writing specs, writing evals, regeneration workflow, observability, and provenance/version-control. Only include practices the blog actually states or clearly implies — do not invent practices and attribute them to the blog. If the blog is thin on concrete practice in some area, say so explicitly in one line rather than padding.
- Then a "Post index" section: one line per post — title plus a one-sentence gist — so a future agent can tell what exists without re-reading the mirror.
- Total length target: roughly 150-250 lines of markdown. Terse and dense beats comprehensive and flabby. No marketing tone.

FIDELITY RULES:
- Distinguish clearly between what the author claims and what you infer. Inference is allowed only in a final short "Implications for this repo's experiment" section (max 6 bullets), clearly labeled as our interpretation, aimed at a TodoMVC spec-and-evals regeneration experiment.
- If posts contradict or refine each other, note the refinement rather than silently picking one.

Your final message: report the number of posts you found and read, the path of the file you wrote, and any posts you could NOT extract cleanly (truncated, image-only, etc.). Do not paste the whole document into your final message.

description: Build Go eval runner from docs
prompt:
You are building a test runner in Go, inside the repository /Users/matteo/dojo/2026-07-22-phoenix/todomvc.

THIS IS A DOCUMENT-SUFFICIENCY EXPERIMENT. You must build the runner using ONLY these inputs:
- /Users/matteo/dojo/2026-07-22-phoenix/todomvc/evals/DSL.md — the scenario language definition; your primary spec
- /Users/matteo/dojo/2026-07-22-phoenix/todomvc/evals/HARNESS.md — the contract between the runner and app implementations (manifest with start/seed/read commands, execution semantics)
- /Users/matteo/dojo/2026-07-22-phoenix/todomvc/evals/scenarios/*.md — the scenario files your runner must parse and execute
- /Users/matteo/dojo/2026-07-22-phoenix/todomvc/docs/main-screen-template.html and main-screen-template.css — the DOM vocabulary (element classes, structure)

You MUST NOT read any other file in the repository — in particular NOT docs/spec.md, NOT docs/architecture.md, NOT docs/principles.md, NOT docs/handbook.md, NOT README.md, NOT schema/. That is the point of the experiment: the four inputs above claim to be sufficient to build the runner. If you need information they don't contain, do NOT go find it elsewhere and do NOT silently invent it: make a reasonable choice and record the gap.

GAP LOG: create /Users/matteo/dojo/2026-07-22-phoenix/todomvc/evals/runner/QUESTIONS.md. Every time the input documents are ambiguous, contradictory, or silent on something you needed, add an entry: what you needed, where the docs fell short (file + section), what you chose and why. An empty log is a valid outcome. This log is a primary deliverable — it is the list of defects in our durable documents.

WHAT TO BUILD: a Go module at evals/runner/ (its own go.mod; it must not share any other module) implementing everything DSL.md's "Runner obligations" and HARNESS.md's "Execution semantics" demand:
- Discover and parse all scenario files in evals/scenarios/ per the DSL grammar (GIVEN model / GIVEN route / WHEN / THEN page / THEN model / THEN check, model notation with optional #ids, NOTE lines ignored).
- Drive a real browser via CDP using github.com/chromedp/chromedp (Chrome is installed at the standard macOS path; go1.26 is available; run headless).
- Per HARNESS.md: read app/harness.json; run the start command once per run from the app dir, poll the URL until ready; per scenario: fresh browser context, run the seed command (stdin JSON, ALSO for the empty model), navigate to url+route, execute WHEN steps, evaluate THENs (page projection diff, model via the read command, checks).
- Implement the ASCII page projection exactly as DSL.md defines, the full action vocabulary, and the check registry. On page mismatch print expected and actual projections side by side.
- Exit nonzero on any failure; per-scenario pass/fail summary.
- CLI: `go run ./cmd/evals -app <path-to-app-dir>` with sensible defaults (scenarios dir found relative to the evals dir; allow overriding).

VERIFICATION — the real app does not exist yet, so you cannot run the suite against it. Instead:
1. `go build ./...`, `go vet ./...`, and `gofmt -l .` (empty) must be clean.
2. Unit tests for the parser: parse the real scenario files from evals/scenarios/, assert file/scenario counts, section structure, action parsing, model notation with and without ids.
3. Tests for projection and execution against fixture pages you create under the runner's testdata: static HTML shaped per the template in various states (empty app, items active/completed/editing, filters, footer variants), served by a local test HTTP server, driven by real headless chromedp. Fixtures are ephemeral test scaffolding, not an app implementation. You may also create a stub harness (a testdata harness.json whose start serves fixtures and whose seed/read are tiny scripts writing/reading a JSON file) to test the end-to-end orchestration path.
4. `go test ./...` must pass. Note anywhere a test is weaker than you'd like (e.g. write-action verbs are hard to verify against static fixtures — say so rather than fake it).

Do NOT commit anything to git. Do NOT create or modify any file outside evals/runner/.

FINAL REPORT (raw data, no prose flourish): Go file count and rough LOC; build/vet/test results verbatim-ish; the complete QUESTIONS.md entries (tightly summarized); any DSL/HARNESS obligation you could NOT implement or test, and why; anywhere you knowingly made the runner stricter or looser than the documents demand.
Reply with exactly the word PROBE and nothing else. Do not use any tools.
